coredata.dc:title: Reversible AIE self-assembled nanohybrids coordinated by La3+ for ratiometric visual acid phosphatase monitoring and intracellular imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yin, Chenhui
Wu, Meng
Liu, Haochi
Sun, Qijun
Sun, Xue
Niu, Na
Xu, Jiating
Chen, Ligang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence method was established for sensing acid phosphatase (ACP) activity based on La3+ ions coordinated self-assembled carbon nanodots-templated copper nanoclusters. La3+-induced aggregation-induced emission (AIE), reversible competition between adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and La3+, and ACP-induced dephosphorylation are cleverly combined. At 350 nm excitation wavelength, carbon nanodots-templated copper nanoclusters, denoted as nanohybrids, exhibited blue and red fluorescence. However, blue fluorescence intensity (FL) decreased due to static quenching, while red FL enhanced in the nanohybrids due to AIE when adding La3+. Interestingly, La3+-induced nanohybrids self-assembly process could be inhibited by ATP through a stronger interaction between ATP and La3+ relative to carboxyl group and La3+. ACP catalyzed the directed hydrolysis of ATP, thereby dissociating the La3+-ATP complexes. The ratio (F610/F452) had a good correlation with ACP concentration in the range of 0.1–10 U/L, and the detection limit is 0.024 U/L. The introduction of smartphones has successfully implemented the visual analysis of ACP. This study demonstrates that La3+-induced AIE has emerged as a competitive candidate strategy to boost the brightness and stability of CuNCs and their nanohybrids. Furthermore, thanks to the good biocompatibility of La3+-nanohybrids, ACP detection in serums and imaging of HeLa cells have been successfully achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Acid phosphatase
La3+ ions
Ratiometric fluorescence
Visual detection
Intracellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The ACP detection process based on La3+-nanohybrids
Optical properties and AIE behavior of nanohybrids
Ratiometric sensing ACP and mechanism study

Tianjin University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This project was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( 2572021AW42 ), the Project program of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, Tianjin University of Science and Technology , P. R. China (No. TJS202102 ), Fundamental Rearch Funds for Provincial Colleges and Universities in Heilongjiang Province ( 2021-KYYWF-0583 ), 111 Project ( B20088 ), and Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of nanohybrids preparation and ACP detection strategy.
TEM images of (A) PNCDs, (B) nanohybrids. (C) FT-IR spectra. (D) UV–vis spectra. (E) XPS analysis. (F) High resolution of Cu.

(A) Photographs of nanohybrids dissolved in ethanol/water mixed solvent under daylight and UV light, (B) their corresponding fluorescence spectra. (C) Photographs of nanohybrids dissolved in different solvents under daylight and UV light, (D) their corresponding fluorescence spectra. (E) Photograph of solid nanohybrids under daylight and UV light. (F) Fluorescence spectra of nanohybrids after adding various amounts of La3+ (inset: photographs of nanohybrids under UV light).

(A) Schematic illustration of ACP ratiometric fluorescence detection by connecting ACP-induced specific hydrolysis of ATP and ATP-triggered removing La3+ from nanohybrids. (B) Feasibility of nanohybrids sensing strategy in the presence of (a) None, (b) La3+, (c) La3+ +ATP, (d) La3+ +ATP+ACP, (e) ATP, (f) ACP, (g) ATP+ACP. (C) Fluorescence lifetime of CuNCs from nanohybrids in the presence of (a) none, (b) La3+, (c) La3++ATP, (d) La3++ATP+ ACP. (D) Fluorescence lifetime of PNCDs from nanohybrids in the presence of (a) none, (b) La3+, (c) La3++ATP, (d) La3++ATP+ ACP.

Sensing of ACP. (A) Fluorescence spectra toward ACP concentrations. (B) The linear relationship of F610/F452 vs ACP concentrations. (C) The CIE chromaticity of the nanohybrids varies with the ACP concentration. (D) The structure of smartphone assistant platform. (E) Smartphone-based judgment of the RGB value for various amounts of ACP. (F) The linear relationship of R/B vs ACP concentrations. (G) Fluorescence response of sensing platform toward various substances. (H) Fluorescence response of sensing platform toward other coexisting substances in presence of ACP. Ions and small molecules concentration: 300 μM; enzymes activity:10 U/L.

(A) Cell viability values (%) incubated with (a) nanohybrids, (b) La3+ with 100 μg/mL nanohybrids, and (c) ATP for 12 h. (B) Fluorescence microscopy images of HeLa cells with (a) nanohybrids, (b) nanohybrids and 40 μM La3+, (c) nanohybrids and 80 μM La3+, (d) nanohybrids, La3+, and ATP, (e) nanohybrids, La3+, ATP and ACP. (B) Fluorescence intensity ratio of a-e (red channel towards blue channel). Scale bar: 50 µm.

head.ce:title: Reversible AIE self-assembled nanohybrids coordinated by La3+ for ratiometric visual acid phosphatase monitoring and intracellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Key Laboratory of Forest Plant Ecology, Northeast Forestry University, 26 Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, College of Food Science and Engineering, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, 258 Xuefu Street, Jiamusi 154007, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, 258 Xuefu Street, Jiamusi, 154007, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a ratiometric fluorescence method was established for sensing acid phosphatase (ACP) activity based on La3+ ions coordinated self-assembled carbon nanodots-templated copper nanoclusters. La3+-induced aggregation-induced emission (AIE), reversible competition between adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and La3+, and ACP-induced dephosphorylation are cleverly combined. At 350 nm excitation wavelength, carbon nanodots-templated copper nanoclusters, denoted as nanohybrids, exhibited blue and red fluorescence. However, blue fluorescence intensity (FL) decreased due to static quenching, while red FL enhanced in the nanohybrids due to AIE when adding La3+. Interestingly, La3+-induced nanohybrids self-assembly process could be inhibited by ATP through a stronger interaction between ATP and La3+ relative to carboxyl group and La3+. ACP catalyzed the directed hydrolysis of ATP, thereby dissociating the La3+-ATP complexes. The ratio (F610/F452) had a good correlation with ACP concentration in the range of 0.1–10 U/L, and the detection limit is 0.024 U/L. The introduction of smartphones has successfully implemented the visual analysis of ACP. This study demonstrates that La3+-induced AIE has emerged as a competitive candidate strategy to boost the brightness and stability of CuNCs and their nanohybrids. Furthermore, thanks to the good biocompatibility of La3+-nanohybrids, ACP detection in serums and imaging of HeLa cells have been successfully achieved.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Acid phosphatase (ACP) is widely present in animal tissues and body fluids [1]. It is an enzyme that catalyzes the hydrolysis of phosphate monoesters under weakly acidic conditions, and has a great influence on regulating cell functions [2,3]. Evaluating ACP levels has been used for disease diagnosis and cell activity monitoring [4]. It has been reported that significantly elevated ACP levels are associated with a variety of diseases, such as prostate cancer [5] and hyperparathyroidism [6]. Therefore, the establishment of sensitive and reliable techniques for closely monitoring the levels of ACP in the biological system is of great significance. So far, electrochemical methods [7], surface acoustic wave (SAW) sensing [8], colorimetric methods [9] and fluorescence methods [10,11] have been used for ACP monitoring. Among these methods, the colorimetric method lacks high sensitivity, and the electrochemical method requires expensive equipment. Fluorescence has attracted widespread attention because of its advantages of high sensitivity, low cost, time saving, real-time monitoring of living cells, and naked-eye readable signal changes under UV light. However, most fluorescence sensors focusing on fluorescence "turn-off" and "turn-on" are limited because of their high false-positive response and low selectivity [12,13].

Ratiometric fluorescence strategy can reduce these problems to a large extent by recording the ratio of the fluorescence intensities of two different emission signals, and can achieve more accurate and efficient detection, which is more suitable for practical applications. In addition, the color change of ratiometric fluorescent probes is more pronounced, making it easier to visualize analytes. However, it is difficult for the human eyes to distinguish similar colors, which significantly limits the capability of visual analysis. In recent years, smartphone-based fluorescence visual sensing platforms have been designed and adapted to enable in situ analysis of biomolecules [14,15]. The use of smartphones has made fluorescence visual sensing analysis faster, easier, and more accurate. Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), have received extensive attention due to low toxicity, good biocompatibility, easy preparation and chemical modification. Therefore, numerous CDs-based fluorescent probes have been reported for sensing chemical/biochemical substances [16]. Besides, copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) have large Stokes shift, good biocompatibility, and low cost, making them promising biosensing and imaging fluorescence prospects [17,18]. However, the low quantum yield (QY) and poor stability of CuNCs have prevented them from being widely utilized relative to other metal NCs. Therefore, it’s urgent to improve the fluorescence performance and stability of CuNCs and their nanohybrids.

Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) has attracted considerable attention since its discovery by Tang’s groups [19]. As the low-fluorescent species changed from dispersion to aggregate, their fluorescence increased significantly. It has great potential in sensing and bioimaging [20]. Solvents and pH are widely considered as elicitors of AIE [21,22]. Singh et al. reported AIE systems for ATP or other phosphate substrates consisting of tetraphenyl derivatives and high charge density substrates (hexametaphosphate, etc.), which were subsequently used for alkaline phosphatase detection with satisfactory results [31–33]. However, these organic dyes with AIE properties have disadvantages such as easy photobleaching and poor biocompatibility.

Compared with the above-mentioned organic fluorescent dyes, fluorescent nanoparticles have better photostability, water dispersibility and biocompatibility. Some nanomaterials with the AIE effect have been reported among them, such as gold nanoclusters and Ag2S quantum dots (Ag2S QDs) [23,40]. CuNCs have attracted significant interest as fluorescent nanomaterials due to their advantages of large Stokes shift, good biocompatibility, and low cost, but there are few studies on the AIE effect of CuNCs [34]. The substances that induce the AIE effect are crucial for nanomaterials. Some nanomaterials exhibit AIE via electrostatic interactions, for example, positive charge PEI-Ag2S QDs are induced by negative charge heparin to exhibit energetic AIE [23]. Moreover, it has been reported that some lanthanide ions such as Ce3+, Eu3+, Yb3+, and La3+ induced AIE effect of Ag2S QDs, and good results have been obtained [35]. Lanthanide ions can coordinate with carboxyl groups in various ways to induce the AIE effect, exhibiting excellent fluorescence properties accompanied by simplicity and tunability. Therefore, we have reason to speculate that CuNCs and their nanohybrids can also be induced by lanthanide ions to exhibit energetic AIE effects.

In this paper, a novel platform was constructed for sensing ACP based on La3+-induced nanohybrids AIE. Phosphorus, nitrogen doped CDs (PNCDs) were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using Adenosine 5-monophosphate sodium salt as raw material ( Fig. 1). Then, nanohybrids were prepared using PNCDs as template and GSH as reducing agent. Nanohybrids exhibit blue (PNCDs) and red fluorescence (CuNCs). In the presence of La3+, the nanohybrids undergone self-assembly driven by AIE. Interestingly, the La3+-coordinated self-assembled nanohybrid exhibited excellent long-term storage stability and salt tolerance. ATP was able to dissociate La3+ from La3+-nanohybrids, resulting in inhibition of AIE-driven La3+-nanohybrids self-assembly process. This may be due to the strong interaction of the triphosphate group with La3+ to generate significant La3+-ATP complexes. ACP-induced ATP-directed hydrolysis generated products that were difficult to disassemble La3+ from La3+-nanohybrids. Therefore, the ratio (F610/F452) gradually increased with ACP concentration while accompanying ATP. The ratiometric fluorescence method was demonstrated to be practical for the detection of ACP in serum and bioimaging.

The preparation and purification of PNCDs and nanohybrids are detailed in Supplemental information. La3+-nanohybrids were prepared as follows: 50 μL nanohybrids (100 μg/mL) and 200 μL La3+ (80 μM) were incubated at room temperature for 10 min to fabricate La3+-nanohybrids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ACP detection process based on La3+-nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: La3+-nanohybrids (250 μL) were fabricated with 50 μL nanohybrids (100 μg/mL) and 200 μL La3+ (80 μM). ACP solutions of different concentrations were formulated in MES buffer solution. Each of the above solutions (150 μL) was then mixed with 100 μL of ATP solution at 37 °C for 40 min. Then 250 μL La3+-nanohybrids and 500 μL MES buffer solution (10 mM, pH=6) was added. The mixture was shaken thoroughly for 2 min until well mixed and the PL spectrum of different samples were recorded in the excitation of 350 nm. Real sample analysis and smartphone-based visual analysis was described in Supplemental information.
Details of MTT colorimetric assay for assessing the cytotoxicity of La3+-nanohybrids and intracellular imaging of ACP-related reaction are described in Supplemental information.

The morphology of PNCDs and nanohybrids were depicted by TEM. In Fig. 2A, PNCDs are spherical with narrow diameter distribution (2.0–3.8 nm) and a small average size (around 2.6 nm). PNCDs exhibit a monodisperse state and are used as templates for in situ preparation of CuNCs. Compared with PNCDs, the nanohybrids showed lager diameter distribution (2.5–6.0 nm) with a larger average size (around 4.0 nm) in Fig. 2B. Subsequently, the surface composition was characterized to identify the formation of nanohybrids. Fig. 2C depicted the FTIR spectra of PNCDs, GSH, and nanohybrids, respectively. For PNCDs, the peak around 3419 cm−1 belong to stretching vibration of hydroxyl and amino groups. The peaks at 1615 and 1281 cm−1 show stretching vibration of CN and PO, respectively [24]. The bands at 2950 and 1441 cm−1 are attributed to C-H and C-C, indicating that AMP may be mostly carbonized in the hydrothermal process. The surface of the generated PNCDs contain plenty of amino and hydroxyl, making it possible to synthesize nanohybrids in situ using PNCDs as templates. For the nanohybrids, the bands at 3418 and 3278 cm−1 belong to hydroxyl, amino groups, and carboxyl. The bands at 1642, 1367, and 1404 cm−1 belong to CO and COO-. More significantly, the peak at 2523 cm−1 (S-H) disappeared, indicating that the Cu-S bond was formed. The above results indicate the successful synthesis of nanohybrids.

Fig. 2D shows the UV–vis absorption of PNCDs, CuNCs, and nanohybrids. PNCDs have two absorption bands at 255 and 348 nm. The former may come from the π-π * transition of the aromatic sp2 structure [25]. The latter may come from the n-π * transition of CO. The UV–vis absorption spectra of CuNCs show two broad weak absorption bands at 264 and 328 nm, which are attributed to the transition of Cu-S to Cu-Cu hybrid bonding orbitals [26,27]. However, for nanohybrids, two peaks at about 260 nm and 297 nm were exhibited.

The surface of the nanohybrids was depicted by XPS as well. Peaks at 131, 162, 282, 397, 529 and 969 eV belong to P, S, C, N, O and Cu (Fig. 2E), respectively. Depending on the Cu(0) or Cu(I) state, 951.2 and 931.6 eV are attributed to Cu 2p1/2 and Cu 2p3/2 [28], respectively (Fig. 2F). No peak appears at 942.1 eV, indicating the absence of Cu(II) state. These results indicate that Cu(II) is completely reduced and the copper in the nanohybrids mainly exists in the form of Cu(I) or Cu(0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and AIE behavior of nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence properties of nanohybrids were explored. Fig. S1A shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of PNCDs. Under the optimal excitation (350 nm), FL at 452 nm reaches the maximum. Fig. S1B shows the fluorescence excitation and emission spectrum of CuNCs. Under the excitation (350 nm), the CuNCs has the maximum fluorescence emission at 610 nm. Fig. S1C shows nanohybrids has two emission peaks at 452 and 610 nm at 350 nm excitation. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of nanohybrids was almost constant within 60 min of UV light irradiation (Fig. S1D). All of these indicated that nanohybrids were successfully synthesized.

The AIE properties of the nanohybrids were then investigated. When the nanohybrids is dispersed in good solvent (water), a clear blue emission and a weak red emission are observed. But with the addition of poor solvent (ethanol), the red emission increases and the blue emission decreases ( Fig. 3A). This suggests that the red emission is consistent with AIE phenomenon, hence the red emission is referred to here as AIE fluorescence. When the ethanol content is 90 %, the AIE fluorescence decreases, probably because the suspension of nanohybrid sinks to the bottom, reducing the effective concentration of nanohybrid in the detection beam region (Fig. 3B). The effect of different solvents on the fluorescence emission spectra of the nanohybrids was investigated (Fig. 3C and D). As the polarity decreases, the AIE fluorescence gradually increases. This may be caused by the dipole-dipole interaction between the solvent and the nanohybrids [29]. The GSH interaction between solvent molecules and the surface of CuNCs, or the poor dispersion of CuNCs, results in cross-linked aggregates of CuNCs. The aggregation of CuNCs makes the vibration and rotation strongly suppressed. Radiation-free relaxation is then suppressed, resulting in enhanced fluorescence [36]. The digital photos of solid-state nanohybrids are shown in Fig. 3E, and the strong orange-red emission of the solid-state nanohybrid can be clearly observed under UV light. Besides, we found that La3+ can induce the AIE phenomenon. Fig. 3F reveals FL intensity of La3+-nanohybrids significantly increased at 610 nm, while FL intensity at 452 nm decreased. Obviously, the strong luminescence of La3+-nanohybrids at 610 nm originates from the aggregation of CuNCs induced by La3+. In addition, the fluorescence emission peak is slightly blue-shifted, which may be due to the complexation of La3+ and carboxyl from CuNCs (Fig. S2). The influence of La3+ on nanohybrids was investigated. In Fig. S3A, as concentration of La3+ increases, F610/F452 gradually increases and then achieves balance. Thus, the optimal concentration of La3+ is 80 μM. In Fig. S3B, F610/F452 increases rapidly, indicating that the assembly induced by La3+ is spontaneous and rapid. After 2 min, the fluorescence of the system remains stable. Moreover, La3+-nanohybrids has long-time preservation stability, salt tolerance and high QY than nanohybrids detailed in Supplemental information (Fig. S4, Fig. S5). Different types of lanthanide ions have also been investigated for nanohybrids aggregation-induced fluorescence responses (Fig. S11). The order of fluorescence response enhancement effect was La3+>Ce3+>Gd3+>Tb3+>Yb3+>Eu3+>Er3+>Nd3+. Therefore, La3+ ions were selected to were selected to induce nanohybrids for AIE self-assembly, which was subsequently used for ACP detection. The possible mechanism of fluorescence changes was explored and detailed in Supplemental information. These investigations provided the basis for a better understanding of the interaction between ATP and La3+-nanohybrids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric sensing ACP and mechanism study
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the ratio fluorescent probe for the determination of ACP has also been studied. Fig. 4A shows a possible reaction mechanism. ATP, a triphosphate compound, is suitable as a substrate for the detection of ACP. Interestingly, the products generated by ACP catalyzed ATP dephosphorylation are difficult to compete with the effects of La3+ and nanohybrids. Fig. 4B reveals fluorescence changes of nanohybrids in the presence of La3+, ATP or ACP. Based on this characteristic, La3+-nanohybrids can be used to sense ACP.

To further reveal the mechanism, we studied the TEM image, fluorescence lifetime, and Zeta potential. TEM image shows that La3+-nanohybrids return from the original aggregation state to the dispersed state when ATP is added (Fig. S6, Fig. S7). This is due to the stronger force between ATP and La3+. La3+ detaches from the surface of the nanohybrids, and the aggregates reappear in a dispersed state. La3+ was added to the La3+-nanohybrids-ATP system to further explore the role of ATP. When excess La3+ was added immediately, blue fluorescence enhanced and red fluorescence decreased. It can be inferred that the original ATP-induced fluorescence change is due to reversible competition, which leads to the redispersion of the nanohybrids. After that, the fluorescence lifetime changes were explored. After adding ATP, the CuNCs fluorescence lifetime from La3+-nanohybrids decreased from 20.92 μs to 3.32 μs (Fig. 4C). However, after adding ACP, it increased from 3.32 μs to 5.03 μs. This may be due to the redispersion of La3+-nanohybrids, and the less restriction of the rotational vibration in the molecule results in a shorter fluorescence lifetime. The PNCDs fluorescence lifetime from La3+-nanohybrids changed from 3.19 ns to 3.23 ns (Fig. 4D) after adding ATP and changed to 3.20 ns when adding ATP and ACP together. In addition, the Zeta potential of the nanohybrids increased from − 8.61 mV to − 3.19 mV upon adding La3+. The La3+-nanohybrids Zeta potential is reduced to − 6.42 mV after adding ATP and changed to − 3.95 mV when adding ATP and ACP together. It implies that La3+ is removed from the nanohybrids due to the stronger interaction of ATP and La3+, and the nanohybrids is re-dispersed in solution.

The effects of substrate ATP concentration, pH, and reaction time were investigated in order to optimize the La3+-nanohybrids-ATP fluorescence response. The results were detailed in Supplemental information (Fig. S8). Under optimized conditions, detecting ACP experiments were carried out. The fluorescent intensity at 610 nm regularly increased, whereas that of PCNDs at 452 nm decreased by the increased in ACP concentration ( Fig. 5A). Fig. 5B demonstrates the linearity of F610/F452 with ACP concentration within 0.1–10 U/L: Y = 0.1337X + 0.0612 (R2 = 0.995), where Y represents F610/ F452 and X represents ACP concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 0.024 U/L according to 3σ/k (n = 11). Fig. 5C revealed CIE chromaticity of the nanohybrids varies with the ACP concentration.

Fig. 5D shows a smartphone-assisted platform consisting of dark box, holder, UV lamp and smartphone. The distance between the UV light source and detection solution was maintained at 10 cm. The distance between smartphone and detection solution was also maintained at 10 cm. Pictures of the chromaticity changes caused by different amounts of ACP were captured by a smartphone camera. Fig. 5E shows various fluorescence images captured under 365 nm UV light after adding various concentrations of ACP. It can be seen that the color change of CuNCs after interaction with ACP is not obvious (Fig. 5E). However, the nanohybrid exhibited a series of color changes with increasing ACP concentration, from blue to purple, to pink, and finally to red. Multiple color changes facilitate more sensitive visual detection of ACP. Various fluorescence images were collected and the intensity (RGB) of each color channel was further quantified via a mobile app. Analytical models were built based on these data to examine the relationship of color channel intensities to ACP concentrations. Different models are discussed including R, G, B, R/B, R/G, and G/B (Fig. S12). The optimal model is determined by comparing the coefficients of linear regression equations, and the best model is R/B vs ACP concentrations. As shown in Fig. 5E, the R/B value gradually increased with the increase of ACP concentration. The R/B value and ACP concentration showed an exact linear relationship in the range of 0.5–10 U/L., the linear regression equation can be expressed as Y = 0.1102X + 0.4867 (R2 = 0.996), where Y is R/B and X is the concentration of ACP (Fig. 5F). The LOD was estimated to be 0.097 U/L.

In selective evaluation, some coexisting substances that may be derived from biological samples are tested. Other inorganic ions and biomolecules did not cause significant fluorescence changes (Fig. 5G). ACP is the only substance that significantly changes the fluorescence ratio. In Fig. 5H, the response of the reaction system to ACP is essentially the same with the addition of interfering substances. Results indicate that the aforementioned interferents have negligible effects on ACP measurements. This ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibits good selectivity for ACP, mainly ascribed to its unique ability to directed-hydrolysis of ATP.

To verify the practical applicability of the method based on the ratiometric probe, the ability to detect ACP in real samples was examined. A fluorescence spectrophotometer was used to determine ACP concentrations in serum samples. The analytical results of human serum samples are shown in Table S1. The results for unspiked samples measured by fluorometry were 4.73, 7.65, and 3.81 U/L, respectively. The average recovery of ACP was 97.8 %− 102.2 %, and the RSD was less than 4.2 %.

The detection results of proposed method were compared with those of the traditional colorimetric method to examine its accuracy [30]. The results of the traditional colorimetric method in the unspiked samples were 4.71, 7.59 and 3.87 U/L, respectively (Table S1). This proved that their detection results are consistent. However, traditional colorimetric methods rely on the color-developing substance, p-nitrophenol, and the color distinction is not obvious, resulting in insufficient sensitivity.

The constructed method is compared with other methods for detecting ACP, including colorimetric and fluorometric methods ( Table 1). In terms of RSD and recovery, satisfactory results can be obtained in these methods. In terms of LOD, the constructed method has a lower LOD than most colorimetric and fluorometric methods. Although the LOD of constructed method is still higher than that of the few fluorescence methods, the constructed method has a wider linear range. In addition, the ratiometric fluorescence strategy makes the probe resistant to external factors, making the results more reliable. The polychromatic changes of nanohybrids solution enables real samples to be analyzed not only with spectroscopic instruments, but also visually via the smartphone assistant platform.

The proposed ratiometric fluorescence strategy was further used for ACP imaging in HeLa cells. The cytotoxicity of the fluorescent system was first evaluated by the MTT method. Fig. 6A(a) illustrates cell viability of HeLa cells co-incubated with different concentrations of nanohybrids (25–200 μg/mL) for 24 h, indicating that nanohybrids have satisfactorily low cytotoxicity. The cytotoxicity of nanohybrids in the presence of different concentrations of La3+ and ATP was also investigated. Fig. 6A(b) illustrates that cell viability is still high even at La3+ concentrations up to 200 μM. The effect of ATP on HeLa cell viability is shown in Fig. 6A(c). Even at ATP concentrations as high as 300 μM, HeLa cells were up to 92 % active, indicating low cytotoxicity. These results suggest that this fluorescent system has low cytotoxicity and is suitable for imaging of intracellular ACP. Encouraged by the excellent low cytotoxicity and selectivity, ratiometric fluorescence sensing for intracellular ACP imaging was further investigated.

Fig. 6B (a) is a fluorescence image of HeLa cells incubated with nanohybrids for 12 h. The results show that nanohybrids have bright blue fluorescence and weak red fluorescence. After treatment with La3+ for 1 h, the fluorescence signal in the blue channel was attenuated, while the red fluorescence was enhanced (Fig. 6B(b) and (c)), which is consistent with the AIE results of extracellular La3+-driven nanohybrids. After ATP treatment, blue fluorescence recovered and red fluorescence was significantly weakened (Fig. 6B(d)). This is because ATP is more easily combined with La3+ to form a complex La3+-ATP, which induces the release of La3+ from the surface of nanohybrids and hinders the AIE process. Fluorescence images of ACP-treated HeLa cells exhibited intense red fluorescence and relatively weak blue fluorescence compared to untreated cells (Fig. 6B(e)). A possible explanation is that ACP hydrolyzes intracellular ATP, leading to La3+ to re-induce the self-assembly of nanohybrids and generate the AIE process. The fluorescence intensity ratios (Fred/Fblue) of a-e are 0.12, 0.65, 1.1, 0.22, 0.78, respectively. It can be concluded that proposed ratiometric fluorescence strategy is well suited for probing ACP imaging and sensing in living cells.

Novel highly sensitive ratiometric fluorescent strategy was proposed based on carbon dots-templated CuNCs. Nanohybrids with dual emission peaks can be self-assembled by La3+ through carboxyl groups, resulting in AIE fluorescence enhancement of CuNCs and static quenching of PNCDs. More importantly, the as-prepared La3+-nanohybrid exhibits higher QY and longer storage stability than nanohybrid without La3+. La3+-induced AIE, reversible competition between ATP and La3+, and ACP-induced dephosphorylation are cleverly combined for sensing ACP. The ratiometric fluorescence method has the advantages of simplicity, high selectivity and high sensitivity. Under UV light, polychromatic fluorescence changes from blue to violet, pink, or red can also be observed with the naked eye. In addition, the introduction of smartphones makes this fluorescence visual sensing ratiometric fluorescence method easier to operate and more conducive to on-site analysis. This ratiometric fluorescence sensing method has been successfully applied to the detection of ACP in human serum and the imaging of ACP in living cells, which demonstrated its potential role in disease diagnosis related to ACP level.

Chenhui Yin: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Meng Wu: Data curation, Investigation. Haochi Liu: Methodology. Qijun Sun: Investigation. Xue Sun: Conceptualization. Na Niu: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Jiating Xu: Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Ligang Chen: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was funded by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (2572021AW42), the Project program of Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Quality and Health, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, P. R. China (No. TJS202102), Fundamental Rearch Funds for Provincial Colleges and Universities in Heilongjiang Province (2021-KYYWF-0583), 111 Project (B20088), and Heilongjiang Touyan Innovation Team Program (Tree Genetics and Breeding Innovation Team).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132550.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Peptide-induced aggregation of glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters: A new strategy for designing aggregation-induced enhanced emission probes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.G. You, W.L. Tseng, Peptide-induced aggregation of glutathione-capped gold nanoclusters: A new strategy for designing aggregation-induced enhanced emission probes, Anal. Chim. Acta 1078 (2019) 101–111, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2019.05.069.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chenhui Yin is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His major is biology and his research direction is biological analysis.

Meng Wu is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her major is biology and her research direction is chemical biology.
Haochi Liu is a Doctor at Tianjin University of Science and Technology. Her research direction is fluorescent probes and their visualization applications.

Qijun Sun is a Doctor candidate at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His major is biology and his research direction is chemical biology.
Xue Sun is a teacher at Jiamusi University. Her research direction is fluorescent probes and their visualization applications.

Na Niu is an associate professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. Her main research direction is fluorescent probes and their practical applications.

Jiating Xu is a professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His main research directions are rare earth luminescent multifunctional nanomaterials and nanobiological agents.

Ligang Chen is a professor at the College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization at Northeast Forestry University. His main research directions are fluorescence probes and molecular imprinting techniques.
